title: Logon from a Risky IP Address
detectionType: Identity / Activity policy — risky IP logon
description: An activity policy flagged an authentication originating from an IP address with poor reputation (anonymizer, hosting, blocklisted, or otherwise risky). 'Risky' is a reputation label, not a verdict. The triage question is whether this authentication was performed by the legitimate user; a risky IP has many benign explanations (VPN, Tor, mobile CGNAT, travel, cloud workspace, freshly-relisted address) as well as malicious ones (account takeover from anonymized infrastructure).

intake.title: Intake and capture core facts
intake.instructions:
Confirm this is a new alert (not a duplicate of an open case) and record the identifiers you will reason about throughout.

- Note the identity: user principal / account, role, and typical work location/timezone.
- Note the source IP, plus the geolocation, ASN, and hosting/organization the IP resolves to.
- Note the authentication outcome: success, failure, or success-after-failures.
- Note the target application/resource and the authentication method (password, MFA, token, legacy protocol).
- Record the event timestamp and the reporting identity source.

enrich-ip.title: Characterize the risky IP
enrich-ip.instructions:
Establish WHY the IP is considered risky, because the reason changes how much weight it carries.

- Gather reputation from a threat-intel capability: is it flagged for anonymization (VPN/proxy/Tor), hosting/datacenter, brute-force/scanning, malware C2, or spam?
- Classify the IP type: residential ISP, mobile/cellular (carrier-grade NAT), commercial VPN, cloud/hosting provider, Tor exit node, or corporate egress.
- Check the age/recency of the risk listing: a freshly-listed address may reflect a prior unrelated tenant on a recycled/dynamic IP.
- Determine whether this IP or ASN is one your organization or users legitimately use (corporate VPN egress, sanctioned cloud workspace, known jumpbox).

evidence.label: Why is it risky? (anonymizer / hosting / scanner / C2 ...)
IP type (residential / mobile / VPN / hosting / Tor / corp)
Is this IP/ASN legitimately used by us?

auth-success?.title: Did the authentication actually succeed?
auth-success?.instructions: A failed logon from a risky IP is exposure/noise; a successful one is a potential account takeover. Route accordingly. 'Success after failures' counts as success and is itself a suspicious pattern to carry forward.

options.label: Failed — no successful session established
options.note: Attempted access only; assess pattern and exposure.

failed-branch.title: Assess the failed-authentication pattern
failed-branch.instructions:
No session was established, but the attempt still tells you something about targeting.

- Determine volume and cadence: a single failure vs. repeated attempts (password spray / brute force / credential stuffing).
- Check whether the same source IP or ASN targeted other accounts in the same window (spray indicator).
- Determine why it failed: wrong password, MFA not satisfied, or conditional-access/policy block — MFA/policy blocking a correct password is more concerning than a wrong password.
- Check whether the account is showing signs of being targeted (prior lockouts, recent password sprays).

evidence.label: Other accounts hit from same IP/ASN?
Failure reason (bad pw / MFA / policy block)

failed-verdict?.title: Does the failed attempt warrant protective action?
failed-verdict?.instructions: Decide whether this is background noise to close, or a targeting pattern that justifies hardening even though no compromise occurred.

options.label: Targeting pattern (spray / brute force / repeated)
options.note: No breach, but the account/IP warrants action.

options.label: Isolated / benign failed attempt
options.note: Single or low-signal failure; close as noise.

user-context.title: Establish the legitimate-user baseline
user-context.instructions:
The session succeeded. Determine whether the legitimate user could plausibly be behind this IP. Work these benign explanations explicitly.

- Travel: is the user known/expected to be traveling, and is the geolocation consistent with plausible travel from their last known location (vs. impossible travel)?
- VPN/privacy tools: does the user use a corporate VPN, a commercial VPN, or Tor that would legitimately egress from a risky/anonymized IP?
- Connection type: could this be mobile/cellular (CGNAT) or a cloud workspace / RDP jumpbox / VDI that legitimately presents a hosting IP?
- Device & session: is the device known/managed and previously seen for this user? Was MFA satisfied by a legitimate factor, or was there MFA fatigue (repeated prompts) before approval?
- Concurrency: are there simultaneous sessions from the user's normal location AND the risky IP (a strong takeover signal)?

evidence.label: Known VPN / Tor / privacy-tool use by user
Mobile CGNAT / cloud workspace / VDI explanation?

post-login.title: Review post-authentication session behavior
post-login.instructions:
Behavior after login separates a benign-but-risky IP from an active takeover, even when context is ambiguous.

- Review actions taken in the session: mailbox rule creation/forwarding, mass file access/download, permission or MFA-method changes, OAuth app consent grants.
- Check for identity-persistence moves: new MFA device registered, password change, recovery info altered.
- Assess whether the activity matches the user's normal behavior and role, or is anomalous in type, volume, or timing.
- Confirm the user's account of the activity where feasible (did they actually log in from this location/tool?).

verdict?.title: What is the verdict on the successful logon?
verdict?.instructions: Weigh IP nature, user baseline, and session behavior together. Confirmed benign explanation + normal behavior = false positive. Impossible travel, unexplained anonymizer, MFA fatigue/anomalous factor, malicious post-login actions, or concurrent sessions = account takeover. If you genuinely cannot resolve it, treat as suspicious and verify with the user out-of-band.

options.label: Confirmed account takeover / unauthorized access
options.note: Malicious behavior or unexplained anonymized access.

user-verify.title: Verify directly with the user (out-of-band)
user-verify.instructions:
Ambiguous case. Reach the user through a trusted channel — not by replying to the flagged session or account — to confirm the activity.

- Use a known phone/secondary channel; do not rely on the possibly-compromised account.
- Ask whether they logged in at that time, from that location, and via any VPN/Tor/mobile/cloud tool.
- If you cannot reach them promptly and any takeover indicator exists, treat as compromise and contain.

verify-result?.title: Did the user confirm the activity as their own?
verify-result?.instructions: Route on the user's confirmation. If unreachable with indicators present, choose containment.

options.label: User denies, or unreachable with indicators

contain-identity.title: Contain the identity
contain-identity.instructions:
Credible account takeover. Contain the identity before scoping and handoff.

- Revoke active sessions/tokens for the account and force re-authentication.
- Reset the password and require a fresh, trusted MFA re-enrollment; remove any attacker-registered MFA methods.
- Reverse persistence: delete malicious mailbox rules/forwarding, revoke illicit OAuth grants, restore recovery settings.
- Block or restrict the source IP where appropriate and record every action with timestamps.

scope-identity.title: Scope the compromise
scope-identity.instructions:
Determine what the attacker reached before handoff.

- Build a timeline of the attacker's session(s): first access to latest activity, and everything done in between.
- Enumerate data accessed, exported, or shared, and any messages sent from the account.
- Check for lateral movement: other accounts contacted, internal phishing, or shared resources touched.
- Identify other accounts authenticating from the same IP/ASN (shared-campaign indicator) and whether any secrets/credentials were exposed.

escalate-identity.title: Escalate to incident response
escalate-identity.instructions:
Confirmed identity compromise with containment and scope established. Hand off with a complete package.

- Ensure identity, IP characterization, session timeline, data exposure, and containment actions are all recorded above.
- Assign incident severity per your matrix and notify the on-call IR owner.
- Preserve identity/audit logs before retention windows expire.
- Transfer ownership; eradication, further notifications, and recovery proceed under the IR playbook.
escalate-identity.summary: Confirmed account takeover via risky-IP logon; identity contained, scoped, and escalated to IR.

harden-close.title: Close — failed attempt, protective hardening applied
harden-close.instructions:
No compromise occurred, but the account/IP showed a targeting pattern worth acting on.

- Document the pattern (spray/brute force/repeated) and confirm no successful session exists.
- Apply proportionate hardening: block the source IP/ASN, confirm the account is protected by MFA, and consider a precautionary credential reset if the password may be known.
- Note whether other targeted accounts need the same treatment.
- Propose detection tuning if the source is now blocked.
harden-close.summary: Failed risky-IP logon with targeting pattern; no breach. Source blocked and account hardening applied.

close-fp-failed.title: Close — false positive (isolated failed attempt)
close-fp-failed.instructions:
An isolated, low-signal failed authentication from a risky IP with no targeting pattern and no session.

- Document that no session was established and no pattern was found.
- If the IP is a chronically noisy source unrelated to real targeting, propose tuning to reduce recurrence.
close-fp-failed.summary: Isolated failed logon from a risky IP; no session, no pattern. Closed as false positive.

close-fp-success.title: Close — false positive (legitimate user)
close-fp-success.instructions:
The successful logon is explained by legitimate user activity (corporate/commercial VPN, Tor by a privacy-conscious user, travel, mobile CGNAT, or a cloud workspace/VDI), device and behavior are consistent, and the user confirmed where applicable.

- Document the specific benign explanation and the confirming evidence (baseline, device, user confirmation).
- If this pattern is expected to recur for this user or IP/ASN (e.g. a sanctioned VPN egress), propose scoped tuning/allow-listing so it does not re-alert without cause.
close-fp-success.summary: Risky-IP logon explained by legitimate user activity (VPN/travel/mobile/cloud); behavior normal. Closed as false positive.
